content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 15, 2019

Anthony Taylor
City Attorney
City of Cypress
18881 Von Karman Ave., Ste. 1700
Irvine, CA 95612

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-19-217

Dear Mr. Taylor:

       This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember Jon Peat
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

       Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

       Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 7), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

       May Cypress City Councilmember Peat take part in a decision to approve a conditional use
permit application submitted by the Cypress School District, given that the Councilmember's
spouse is a compensated member of the School District Board of Trustees?

CONCLUSION

       Yes, the Councilmember may take part in the decision to approve the conditional use
permit, given that his spouse's government salary and benefits do not qualify as "income" under the
Act, and the decision would have no reasonably foreseeable effect on the Councilmember's
personal finances.


1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-217
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The City Council for the City of Cypress ("City") is composed of five members, including
Councilmember Jon Peat, who has served on the City Council since 2014. He was re-elected to his
second term in November of 2018 and that term will expire in November of 2022. Bonnie Peat,
Councilmember Peat's spouse, has served as an elected board member ("Trustee") of the Board of
Trustees ("Board") for the Cypress School District ("District") since November of 2018. Her term
of office will expire in 2022. Trustee Peat receives a $240 monthly stipend for attending all regular
and special Board meetings, a $30,000 term life insurance policy, and is eligible to receive health
benefits (however, she has currently elected to not receive health insurance through the District).

The District owns certain real property located within the City ("Site"). It is anticipated that
the District will submit a conditional use permit application ("CUP") to the City for the purpose of
developing the Site in a manner that would increase the value of the land. The City Council will, in
the future, consider the CUP at a public hearing and decide whether to approve or deny the CUP.
The City Council is the final deciding authority for the CUP. The City Council's decision on the
CUP and the development of the Site would have no financial effect upon the compensation
received by Trustee Peat.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act provides "[n]o public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know has a financial
interest." Section 87103 specifies that "[a] public official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family," or on certain economic interests. Among those
enumerated economic interests is "[a]ny source of income . . . aggregating five hundred dollars
($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made." (Section 87103(c).) A public official also has an
interest in his or her own personal finances, which includes the finances of his or her immediate
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

Regulation 18701(a) provides that "[a] financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to
be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a
governmental decision before the official or the official's agency." Regulation 18701(b) provides
the standard for determining the reasonable foreseeability of a financial effect when a financial
interest is not explicitly involved in a decision. Regulation 18701(b) provides, "[a] financial effect
need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably
foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable."

Under Section 82030(b)(2), "income" does not include "[s]alary and reimbursement for
expenses or per diem, and social security, disability, or other similar benefit payments received
from a state, local, or federal government agency . . . ."

File No. A-19-217
Page No. 3

Per Regulation 18702.5(a), "[a] personal financial effect means the financial effect of a
governmental decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family.
The financial effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive
a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision."

Here, the District does not qualify as a source of income economic interest under the Act
because, as government salary and benefits, the compensation that his spouse receives does not
meet the definition of "income" under Section 82030(b)(2). Similarly, it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the approval of the CUP or the development of the Site would affect
Councilmember Peat's personal finances, including the compensation received by Trustee Peat.
Accordingly, the decision before the City Council does not present a potential disqualifying conflict
of interest under the Act, and Councilmember Peat may take part in the CUP decision.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:sal

sections.question:
May Cypress City Councilmember Peat take part in a decision to approve a conditional use 
permit application submitted by the Cypress School District, given that the Councilmember's 
spouse is a compensated member of the School District Board of Trustees?
sections.conclusion:
Yes, the Councilmember may take part in the decision to approve the conditional use 
permit, given that his spouse's government salary and benefits do not qualify as "income" under the 
Act, and the decision would have no reasonably foreseeable effect on the Councilmember's 
personal finances.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 of the Act provides, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest." Section 87103 specifies that "[a] public official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is_ reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a

enumerated economic interests is "[ a ]ny source of income ... aggregating five hundred dollars

interest in his or her own personal finances, which includes the finances of his or her immediate

Regulation 18701 (a) provides that"[ a] financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to 
be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a

foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not 
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable." 
Under Section 82030(b)(2), "income" does not include "[s]alary and reimbursement for 
expenses or per diem, and social security, disability, or other similar benefit payments received 
from a state, local, or federal government agency .... "  
Per Regulation 18702.S(a), "[a] personal financial effect means the financial effect of a 
governmental decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family. 
The financial effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive 
a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision." 
Here, the District does not qualify as a source of income economic interest under the Act

foreseeable that the approval of the CUP or the development of the Site would affect

Accordingly, the decision before the City Council does not present a potential disqualifying conflict 
of interest under the Act, and Councilmember Peat may take part in the CUP decision.

CONCLUSION: Yes, the Councilmember may take part in the decision to approve the conditional use
